title: Put-Call Parity

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: Consider European call price c, European put price p (same strike K, same maturity T) on a non-dividend-paying stock with current price S. Risk-free rate r.
rules: Both options are European (only exercisable at T).
setup.question: State and prove put-call parity.

learningObjectives: Construct two portfolios A and B that have identical payoffs at maturity.
Apply no-arbitrage to conclude they have the same value at any t < T.
Recognize the parity formula: c + K * exp(-rT) = p + S.

stages.label: Construct the two portfolios
stages.prompt: Consider Portfolio A = long call + zero-coupon bond paying K at T. Portfolio B = long put + 1 share of stock. Compute the payoff of each at maturity (when S_T is observed).

options.text: A: max(S_T - K, 0) + K = max(S_T, K).  B: max(K - S_T, 0) + S_T = max(S_T, K).  IDENTICAL.

stages.explanation: The call profits when S_T > K; bonus K from the bond gives total max(S_T - K, 0) + K = max(S_T, K). The put profits when S_T < K; plus owning the stock gives max(K - S_T, 0) + S_T = max(S_T, K). IDENTICAL.

stages.label: Apply no-arbitrage
stages.prompt: Two portfolios with identical payoffs at every state at time T must have...

options.text: ...the same value at every t < T (otherwise arbitrage: short the expensive one, long the cheap one).
...the same expected return but different prices.

stages.explanation: By no-arbitrage, two portfolios with the SAME terminal payoffs must have the SAME current price. Otherwise you could short the expensive one and long the cheap one for a risk-free profit.

stages.label: Write the parity
stages.prompt: PV of Portfolio A = c + K * exp(-rT). PV of Portfolio B = p + S. Setting equal:
stages.explanation: The bond's PV today (price to receive K at T): K * exp(-rT). Stock and call/put prices are spot prices. The parity equation: c + K * exp(-rT) = p + S.

stages.label: Commit and use the parity
stages.prompt: Rearrange to express c - p in terms of stock and bond. What's the financial interpretation?

options.text: c - p = S - K * exp(-rT). The combination 'long call + short put' replicates a forward contract with delivery price K. So c > p iff S > K * exp(-rT) (i.e., K is below the forward price).

stages.explanation: The 'risk reversal' c - p replicates a synthetic forward. When K equals the forward price S * exp(rT), call equals put (at-the-money in forward terms). When K < forward, call > put.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize: with continuous dividend yield y, the parity becomes c + K * exp(-rT) = p + S * exp(-yT). Verify the stock-side adjustment.
For AMERICAN options, why is the parity an INEQUALITY rather than an equation? (Hint: American options can be exercised early, breaking the payoff replication.)
Use the parity to compute call price given put price.
